title: Improving Menopause Care for Veterans Act of 2025
short_title: Improving Menopause Care for Veterans Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill tells a government watchdog group to study how well the Department of Veterans Affairs helps women veterans going through menopause. The watchdog must write a public report about this within eighteen months. After the report comes out, the VA Secretary has six months to make a plan that follows the report's suggestions to give better menopause care to women veterans.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Health.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Improving Menopause Care for Veterans Act of two thousand and twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Comptroller General Study and Report on Menopause Care Furnished by Department of Veterans Affairs
sections.text:
The Comptroller General of the United States (a government watchdog who reviews how federal agencies spend money and provide services) must do a study about medical care that the Department of Veterans Affairs provides to women veterans going through menopause. This includes care for perimenopause (the time before menopause when hormone levels start to change), genitourinary syndrome of menopause (problems with the bladder and genital areas caused by menopause), and all stages of menopause. The study will look at medical services provided under two specific laws that govern veteran healthcare.

Within eighteen months after this law is passed, the Comptroller General must publish a public report about this study. The report must include several specific things. It must describe what menopause care the Department of Veterans Affairs currently provides. It must review the Department's rules and procedures for training doctors, diagnosing and treating menopause-related conditions in women veterans, and sending veterans to doctors outside the VA system when needed.

The report must also evaluate whether veterans can easily get team-based care from different types of medical specialists to treat menopause effects. It must review how well the Department teaches veterans about available menopause care, including treatment options and their benefits and risks. The report must evaluate the quality of menopause care by looking at feedback from veterans, checking how well the treatments work, and seeing if the Department has ways for veterans to report problems with their care.

The report must review how the Department uses research from within the VA or from other organizations to improve menopause care, including developing new treatments or procedures. Finally, the report must include recommendations for making menopause care better.

Within six months after the Comptroller General publishes this report, the Secretary of Veterans Affairs must give Congress a strategic plan. This plan must explain how the Department will put the report's recommendations into action, improve the quality of menopause care, and make it easier for veterans to get menopause care.
